headline: Webb Telescope Reveals Hidden Chemistry of a Hellish Lava Planet
meta_description: NASA's James Webb Telescope discovers a hydrogen-rich atmosphere on 55 Cancri e, a lava planet 41 light years away, revealing secrets about extreme rocky world…
summary_short: Astronomers using the James Webb Space Telescope have discovered unexpected chemistry in the atmosphere of 55 Cancri e, a molten rock world orbiting its star every 17 ho…
body:
Forty-one light years from Earth orbits one of the galaxy's most inhospitable worlds: 55 Cancri e, a rocky planet so close to its star that its surface remains perpetually molten. Yet this hellish world is teaching us something remarkable about how extreme planets form and evolve.

Using NASA's James Webb Space Telescope, researchers observed five eclipses of 55 Cancri e and discovered an atmosphere unlike what scientists had predicted. Rather than being dominated by carbon monoxide and carbon dioxide, the planet contains surprisingly large amounts of hydrogen alongside carbon monoxide and modest amounts of carbon dioxide.

This hydrogen-rich atmosphere reveals something profound: it acts as a window into the planet's molten interior. A planet's chemical composition—what scientists call its redox state—determines what gases escape from its depths. On 55 Cancri e, hydrogen strongly dominates over oxygen, suggesting the planet's interior chemistry is fundamentally different from what models had assumed.

Even more intriguingly, the five observations showed variations that may indicate volcanic outgassing or temporary clouds forming from materials released by the planet's interior. These clouds could briefly cool the surface before dissipating.

As astronomers continue studying 55 Cancri e and other lava worlds, each observation pulls back the curtain on planetary formation itself, revealing how rocky worlds evolve under extreme conditions.
